title: YouTube — Upload & Streaming — System Design
description: Design the upload, transcoding, and streaming pipeline for a video platform: accept large uploads reliably, process them into multiple streaming-ready renditions asynchronously, and deliver them to millions of viewers via a CDN. The themes are resumable upload, async transcode fanout, adaptive streaming, and the metadata/blob/CDN split.

nodes.label: Creator (Uploader)
nodes.description: Uploads source video; later sees processing status.

nodes.label: Upload Service
nodes.description: Issues resumable upload sessions and accepts chunks.

nodes.label: Raw Upload Store
nodes.description: Durable storage for the original uploaded file.

nodes.label: Video Metadata Service
nodes.description: Owns video records, status, and rendition manifests.

nodes.label: Metadata Store
nodes.description: Authoritative video metadata and processing state.

nodes.label: Transcode Job Queue
nodes.description: Pending transcode/processing jobs, fanned out per rendition.

nodes.label: Transcode Workers
nodes.description: Decode source, encode renditions, segment for streaming.

nodes.label: Rendition Store
nodes.description: Durable storage of encoded renditions + HLS/DASH segments.

nodes.label: Playback Service
nodes.description: Returns the streaming manifest and signed segment URLs.

nodes.label: View-Count Pipeline
nodes.description: Ingests and dedups view events into counts.

functional: Upload a video file (potentially many GB) reliably, resuming after interruptions.
Transcode each upload into multiple resolutions/bitrates (renditions).
Stream videos with adaptive bitrate to many concurrent viewers.
Show creators processing status; play once ready.
nonFunctional: Reliable uploads that survive flaky connections (resumable).
Processing is async — upload returns fast; playback becomes available when renditions are ready.
Durable storage of source and renditions.
Scale ingestion (hundreds of hours uploaded per minute) and serving (billions of watch hours) independently.
Access control on playback: public, unlisted, private, and geo-restricted videos enforced without breaking CDN cacheability.

Out of scope but acknowledged: content validation/moderation (malware scan, policy, copyright matching) as a pre-publish hook; operational SLOs (playback start latency, rebuffering ratio, transcode latency, upload success rate).

capacity.label: Upload ingress
capacity.note: Assumption: ~100 MB per source-minute ≈ 13 Mbps average source bitrate — varies widely (4K HDR is multiples higher, 480p talking-head far lower). 300 hr/min ≈ 5 hr/s × ~6 GB/hr ≈ 0.5 GB/s sustained. Motivates resumable chunked upload (step 2); size headroom for bitrate spread.

capacity.label: Transcode multiplier
capacity.note: Assumption: a 5-8 rung ladder (240p..4K × codecs), each roughly real-time-ish to encode on the target hardware. The multiplier swings with codec choice (AV1/VP9 are far costlier than H.264) and how many rungs you ship. One uploaded hour ≈ several compute-hours → segment-parallel fanout (step 4).

capacity.label: Worker fleet / backlog
capacity.value: thousands of cores; queue depth = burst buffer
capacity.note: 300 src-hr/min × ~6x ≈ 1800 transcode-hr/min ≈ 30 transcode-hr/s of work; spikes buffer as queue depth and autoscale the fleet (steps 3, 7).

capacity.label: Storage growth
capacity.note: Assumption: immutable source dominates; an efficient segmented ladder adds ~0.5-1x on top, but this is highly sensitive to codec efficiency, rung count, and retention policy (drop rarely-watched rungs and it shrinks). Petabytes/day of net growth → cost tiering (step 7).

capacity.label: CDN edge QPS
capacity.value: millions of segment req/s; hit ratio >95% (assumed)
capacity.note: Assumption: 2-6s segments, so each viewer pulls ~1 segment every few seconds; millions of concurrent viewers ⇒ millions of fetches/sec. The >95% edge-hit assumption holds for head content but degrades for cold/long-tail catalogs — sizes origin-miss bandwidth below (step 5).

capacity.label: Origin miss bandwidth
capacity.value: <5% of edge traffic (assumes >95% hit), skewed to long tail
capacity.note: Assumption: directly the complement of the edge hit ratio above — a 95% hit ratio means ~5% reaches origin, but a colder catalog or a thundering-herd premiere pushes it higher. Hot videos cache; cold/long-tail content drives origin pulls. Sizes origin-shield and pre-warm decisions (step 5).

capacity.label: View-event throughput
capacity.note: Assumption: the range depends on how often the player emits (one start event vs periodic watch heartbeats) and concurrent-viewer count. Either way it's far higher than the metadata write rate — must be aggregated async, not a synchronous counter increment (step 6).

capacity.label: Read:write
capacity.note: Read fanout lives in the CDN; metadata writes (status, view-count deltas) stay small.

api.description: Start a resumable upload session only — no bytes are transferred here. Idempotency-Key header dedupes retried session creates; returns the session handle and chunk part size. The video is created in 'uploading' state; transcode is not enqueued until POST /complete.
Upload one chunk. Content-Range gives the byte offset; the server stores the part, records its checksum/ETag, and is idempotent on re-sent parts (same partNumber + checksum is a no-op).
Resume probe: returns how much the server has and which parts it holds, so the client knows where to continue after an interruption. 409 if client- and server-side total_bytes disagree.

Finalize the upload — this is where the upload lifecycle completes and processing begins. The client sends the ordered part list; the server verifies all parts and total length, assembles/commits the immutable source object, flips the video from 'uploading' to 'processing', and enqueues transcode jobs. Idempotent — a retried complete returns the same videoId. The '202 processing' lives here, after the bytes are safely committed, not on session start.

Control plane: the playback service authorizes the viewer (handles private/unlisted/geo), then returns the manifest plus short-TTL tokenized segment URLs (or a signed cookie). The player fetches segments directly from the CDN — the playback service is never in the segment data path.
api.request: (id in path; viewer auth in header)

api.description: Creator polls processing status. Status distinguishes uploading, processing, ready_to_watch (at least one rendition playable), fully_processed (whole ladder done), and failed; per-rendition state surfaces a single failed rendition without blocking the others.

Ingest a view event (fire-and-forget, batched by the player). The pipeline dedupes by (video, session) within a window and only counts once watched_seconds passes the threshold; request_id makes retries idempotent.

dataModel.name: videos
dataModel.note: Authoritative video record and processing state.

dataModel.name: access_policies
dataModel.note: Playback authorization state the playback service reads before minting a token: geo allow/deny lists and, for private videos, the share ACL. A private video requires an authorization check against creator-owned ACL entries; public/unlisted skip the ACL check.

fields.name: acl_subject_id
fields.type: bigint (user/group granted access; private videos only)

dataModel.name: renditions
dataModel.note: One row per encoded rendition of a video. manifest_key is the manifest root (HLS/DASH) from which the individual segment objects are derived — segments are addressed relative to that manifest, so no per-segment row is needed.

dataModel.name: upload_sessions
dataModel.note: Resumable-upload bookkeeping; one row per in-flight upload.

fields.name: source_key
fields.type: committed raw object key (set on complete)

dataModel.name: upload_parts
dataModel.note: One row per uploaded chunk, for resume reconciliation and idempotent re-sends.

dataModel.name: rendition_tasks
dataModel.note: Per-segment transcode work and the durable source of truth for the fanout (in MetaDB). Workers claim a row by atomic CAS on (state, lease_until); JobQ messages are only scheduling wake-ups, so duplicate deliveries and crashes are reconciled against this table, not the queue.

dataModel.name: view_events
dataModel.note: Raw ingested view events (short-retention); aggregated into videos.view_count. Authoritative analytics/monetization keep their own longer-retention store.

technologyChoices.concern: Upload ingress, resumable sessions, and admission control
tradeoff: Proxying uploads through the application edge gives tight quota, auth, checksum, and resume control but burns expensive app bandwidth on multi-GB files.

Direct-to-object-storage multipart/resumable upload with signed session tokens removes most application bandwidth, but the token broker must encode policy, part size, expiry, and idempotency rules correctly.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A managed object-store upload protocol with signed URLs removes the need to keep long-lived upload connections open through the upload service.

technologyChoices.concern: Raw source and rendition object storage
tradeoff: Object storage is the natural fit for immutable source masters, manifests, and segment objects because it is durable, cheap, and CDN-friendly.
Keeping source masters in a colder tier lowers cost but slows re-transcode and disaster recovery workflows.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: Lifecycle policies and cross-region replication remove much of the hand-built archival, retention, and copy-job machinery.

technologyChoices.concern: Video metadata, upload sessions, and policy state
tradeoff: Relational stores make video status, upload session reconciliation, and access-policy updates easy to reason about.
Wide-column or managed NoSQL stores absorb hot upload-session and status-write volume better, but push more joins and consistency checks into service logic.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A managed autoscaling store with conditional writes and TTLs removes most manual sharding and cleanup work for upload_sessions and upload_parts.

technologyChoices.concern: Transcode job queue and task wake-ups
tradeoff: A durable queue is enough when messages are wake-ups and rendition_tasks in MetaDB owns task state, leases, retries, and dead-letter decisions.
A log or stream is better when many downstream consumers need replay of video lifecycle events, but it adds partition and retention management.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: Managed queues with DLQ, visibility timeout, and consumer autoscaling remove much of the broker operations burden from step 3.

tradeoff: Self-hosted FFmpeg on a container fleet gives precise control over codecs, per-title ladders, and GPU/CPU scheduling.
Managed batch/workflow/transcoder services reduce fleet and retry plumbing, but can constrain codec features, job packing, and per-video optimization.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A managed transcoder or workflow engine removes much of the custom lease renewal, retry orchestration, and fanout scheduling code.

technologyChoices.concern: Packaging, manifests, and adaptive streaming formats
tradeoff: Owning the packaging toolchain makes HLS/DASH manifest versioning, segment duration, codec ladders, and backward-compatible re-packaging explicit.
Managed media packaging gets a standard ABR pipeline running quickly, but may hide details that matter for long-tail storage cost and player compatibility.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A managed packaging service removes much of the custom manifest generation and segment-layout glue.

technologyChoices.concern: CDN edge delivery, origin shield, and signed playback tokens
tradeoff: Managed CDNs provide global cache footprint, origin shield, TLS, and signed URL support without owning edge hardware.
Self-hosted edge caches can reduce cost at extreme scale or inside ISP peering, but operationally shift cache fill, purge, routing, and abuse protection onto the platform team.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: High CDN hit ratio and origin shield make the playback service and object store irrelevant to most segment requests.

technologyChoices.concern: Playback authorization, signed URLs, and key management
tradeoff: Short-TTL signed URLs keep private and geo-restricted videos cacheable because the CDN validates the token while the playback service stays in the control plane.
Central policy engines simplify private-share and geo checks, but token keys and revocation paths become operationally critical.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: Edge-token validation removes the need to route every segment fetch through the playback service.

technologyChoices.concern: View-event stream processing and approximate counters
tradeoff: Streaming aggregation handles high-volume view starts and heartbeats with dedup windows, late arrivals, and eventual public count updates.
A dedicated analytical store is better for authoritative monetization and fraud analysis, while a hot KV/counter store is enough for user-facing approximate counts.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A managed stream processor with checkpoints and windowing removes most custom consumer offset, replay, and late-event bookkeeping.

technologyChoices.concern: Observability, QoE analytics, and cost telemetry
tradeoff: System metrics catch queue age, worker saturation, origin misses, and upload errors; QoE telemetry catches startup latency and rebuffering that infrastructure health can miss.
Columnar analytics are the right back end for per-ISP, per-codec, and per-CDN cost/performance analysis, but are not a replacement for low-latency alerting.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: Managed metrics, tracing, and log pipelines remove much of the custom collection and retention infrastructure.

patterns.name: Resumable chunked upload
patterns.what: Upload in chunks tracked by offset so an interrupted upload resumes instead of restarting.

patterns.name: Async processing pipeline
patterns.what: Accept the upload, return fast, and do heavy work via a job queue and workers.
patterns.whenToUse: Expensive processing (transcoding) that must not block the uploader.
patterns.group: Decouple Processing with a Job Queue

patterns.name: Transcode fanout
patterns.what: Generate many renditions per video as independent parallel jobs.
patterns.whenToUse: When each source needs multiple resolutions/codecs.

patterns.name: Adaptive bitrate streaming
patterns.what: Segment renditions and let the player switch quality to match bandwidth (HLS/DASH).
patterns.whenToUse: Smooth playback across varying networks and devices.

patterns.name: Metadata/blob/CDN split
patterns.what: Keep small metadata transactional, large media in object storage, and serve via CDN.
patterns.whenToUse: Media systems with huge blobs and global read fanout.

patterns.name: Async approximate counting
patterns.what: Count views via a dedup'd event pipeline rather than a synchronous counter.
patterns.whenToUse: High-volume counters tolerant of slight lag.

steps.title: 1. Naive: One-Shot Upload, Serve the Original File (the baseline)
description: Start with the simplest thing that works. The creator uploads the file in a single request; the service stores that one file; viewers are handed that exact original file to play.

This is correct for a tiny demo — and it exposes the problems the rest of the design exists to solve. A multi-GB upload in one request fails on any network hiccup and must restart from zero. The original file is a single resolution/codec, so a phone on 3G and a TV on fibre get the same too-big-or-too-small stream with no adaptation. And one origin can't serve the same multi-GB file to millions of concurrent viewers.
So the baseline motivates everything that follows: resumable chunked upload, asynchronous transcoding into multiple renditions, adaptive streaming, and CDN delivery.

view.caption: The creator uploads to the upload service, which stores one source file in the raw upload store; viewers are handed that exact original file to play — no transcoding, no CDN.

steps.decisionPrompt: You accept a 4 GB upload in one request and hand viewers that same file. What breaks first: the upload, the playback on a phone, or the origin serving millions of viewers?
whyNow: Establishing the one-shot baseline first makes it obvious why upload must be resumable, why processing must produce multiple renditions asynchronously, and why serving must go through a CDN — each later step removes a specific failure from here.

recap.after: Single-request upload, one stored file served directly to viewers.
recap.newRisk: Large uploads can't survive interruptions, one rendition can't adapt to viewer bandwidth, and a single origin can't serve millions of concurrent viewers.

traps.trap: Shipping one-shot upload + serve-the-original as 'good enough'.
traps.why: A single failed request wastes a multi-GB transfer, a lone rendition rebuffers on weak networks, and the origin melts under viewer fanout.
traps.instead: Resumable chunked upload, async transcode into a rendition ladder, adaptive streaming, and CDN delivery.

steps.title: 2. Resumable Chunked Upload
description: Source files can be many GB and connections drop. Start an upload session (idempotency-keyed so a retried create doesn't fork a second session) and accept the file as fixed-size parts tracked by byte offset, so a dropped connection resumes from the last received part instead of restarting.

The session is a small state machine: each PUT part records its offset, size, and a checksum/ETag; a re-sent identical part is a no-op (idempotent), and the server reconciles received_bytes from the parts it actually holds. To resume, the client does GET /uploads/{id} to read the server's offset and part list, then continues from there. If client and server disagree on total_bytes, return 409 rather than silently corrupting the assembly.

Only on POST /complete does the server verify all parts and total length, commit the immutable source object, flip the video to 'processing', and enqueue transcode jobs — so processing never starts on a half-uploaded file. Sessions carry expires_at so abandoned uploads are garbage-collected.

Store the source in durable object storage and create the video record in 'uploading' state. The upload service does no processing — it just gets bytes safely to storage. Validate format/size early; reject obviously bad uploads before storing.

view.caption: The upload service writes the source to the raw upload store and registers the video as uploading in the metadata service, which persists status to the metadata store.

steps.decisionPrompt: A 4 GB upload fails at 90%. What should the client do, and what state makes that possible?

concepts.definition: Chunked upload tracked by offset so it resumes after interruption.
concepts.whyItMatters: Avoids re-uploading gigabytes on every network hiccup.
concepts.example: Session records received_bytes=3.6GB; client resumes from there.

whyNow: Reliable ingestion is the first requirement; getting the source durably stored is the precondition for everything downstream.

recap.after: Source durably stored; video in 'uploading' state.
recap.newRisk: The raw source isn't playable — it must be transcoded, and that's slow.

traps.trap: Accepting the whole file in one request.
traps.why: A single failure wastes the entire transfer and ties up a connection for minutes.
traps.instead: Chunked, resumable upload with an offset-tracked session.

steps.title: 3. Decouple Processing with a Job Queue
description: Transcoding is CPU/GPU-heavy and slow — it must not block the upload response. Jobs are enqueued only after the upload is committed (the /complete step), not when the first chunks land, so workers never run against a half-uploaded source. Once committed, enqueue and return immediately; the video stays 'processing' until renditions are ready.
A queue decouples ingestion rate from processing capacity: upload spikes buffer as queue depth (the backpressure signal that later autoscales the fleet), and the worker fleet scales independently.

Update video status as renditions complete so creators see progress and playback unlocks per-rendition. Treat the queue as a wake-up signal and the task table as the source of truth (made concrete in step 4): if the metadata write succeeds but the queue publish fails, a reconciler re-enqueues from videos in 'processing' whose rendition_tasks rows have no live lease, so a video can't get stuck never-processed even though a lost queue message would otherwise strand it.

view.caption: The metadata service enqueues a transcode job onto the transcode job queue, from which transcode workers pull work, decoupling processing from the upload path.

steps.decisionPrompt: Why return '202 processing' instead of waiting for transcoding to finish?
whyNow: The async-pipeline decision shapes the whole architecture; it's what lets ingestion and processing scale separately.

concepts.definition: Accept work, persist it, and process it later via a queue + workers.
concepts.whyItMatters: Keeps the user-facing path fast and absorbs bursts.
concepts.example: Upload -> enqueue -> 202; workers transcode minutes later.
concepts.group: Decouple Processing with a Job Queue

recap.after: Upload returns fast; processing scales behind a queue.
recap.newRisk: Each video needs many renditions — the processing work multiplies.

steps.title: 4. Transcode Fanout into Renditions
description: Each source is transcoded into several renditions (e.g. 240p..4K, multiple codecs). Fan these out as independent parallel jobs so a video becomes available rendition-by-rendition.
For long videos, split the source on keyframe boundaries into segments and transcode segments in parallel, then stitch — cutting wall-clock time for a single big upload.

The task state is what makes this a pipeline rather than 'put it in a queue'. Be explicit about ownership: the rendition_tasks table in MetaDB is the durable source of truth, and JobQ messages are only scheduling wake-ups. Each unit of work is a rendition_tasks row keyed by (video_id, rendition, segment_id) with state, attempt count, and a lease_until.

Claiming is an atomic compare-and-set on the row: a worker woken by a queue message sets state pending→leased and lease_until=now+T, and whoever wins the CAS owns it — so duplicate queue deliveries are harmless and the loser just drops its message. If the owner crashes, the lease expires and any worker can re-claim the row the same way; that the queue message was already consumed doesn't matter, because the row, not the message, holds the truth. Output keys are deterministic (derived from the task id), so a re-run overwrites the same object idempotently and partial output from the dead attempt is harmless.

On success the worker sets state=done and acks the message; after a max attempt count a task moves to dead_letter rather than retrying forever. A coordinator (the metadata service here) tracks the per-rendition task set, validates the stitched checksum, and only marks a rendition ready when all its segments are done.
Workers read the source, encode, write renditions to durable storage, and update task/rendition state in metadata.

view.caption: Transcode workers pull jobs from the queue, read the source from the raw upload store, write renditions to the rendition store, and mark each rendition ready in the metadata service.

options.name: Chunk-parallel transcode (split, fan out, stitch) (default)
options.description: The source is split on keyframe boundaries into segments transcoded in parallel by many workers, with a coordinator tracking tasks and stitching the segments back together.
pros: Split the source into segments and transcode them in parallel across many workers, cutting a 3-hour 4K job's wall-clock time dramatically.
Scales horizontally — add workers to go faster, bounded only by fleet size.
Each rendition's segments are produced independently and assembled, fitting segmented streaming formats.
cons: Splitting on keyframe boundaries and stitching without artifacts is fiddly.
A fan-out job needs a coordinator to track all segment tasks and detect stragglers.
More small tasks means more queue and scheduling overhead.

view.caption: Workers pull a job, split the source from the raw store into segments transcoded in parallel, stitch them into the rendition store, and mark renditions ready in metadata.

options.name: One worker transcodes the whole file per rendition
options.description: Each rendition is one task in which a single worker reads the entire source and emits the full output, with renditions running in parallel but no intra-file split.
pros: Dead simple: one task reads the source and emits one rendition end to end.
No splitting/stitching logic and no keyframe-boundary edge cases.
Still parallel across renditions (360p/720p/1080p as separate tasks).
cons: Wall-clock time is bounded by transcoding the full file once — slow for a 3-hour 4K source.
A single failed worker restarts the entire rendition from scratch.
Doesn't use idle fleet capacity to speed up one large video.

view.caption: A single worker per rendition reads the whole source from the raw store and writes the complete output to the rendition store, marking it ready in metadata.

options.name: Tiered: fast low-res first, full ladder later
options.description: Workers transcode one low-resolution rendition first so the video is watchable within seconds, then fill in the rest of the quality ladder in the background.
pros: Transcode one low-res rendition immediately so the video is watchable within seconds, then fill the rest of the ladder in the background.
Greatly improves perceived publish latency for creators and early viewers.
Prioritizes scarce worker time on what unblocks playback.
cons: Early viewers get lower quality until higher renditions finish.
Two-phase job state (ready-to-watch vs fully-processed) complicates metadata.
Still must complete the full ladder, so total compute is unchanged.

view.caption: Workers first produce one low-res rendition into the rendition store so the video is quickly watchable, then fill the rest of the ladder, updating the metadata service.

steps.decisionPrompt: A 3-hour 4K video must process quickly. How do you cut the wall-clock transcode time?

concepts.definition: Producing multiple renditions (and segments) as parallel independent jobs.
concepts.whyItMatters: Parallelism shrinks processing time and lets playback start before all renditions finish.
concepts.example: 8 renditions x N segments = many small jobs across the worker fleet.

whyNow: Transcode fanout is the compute heart of the case; segment-level parallelism is the staff-level optimization interviewers probe.

recap.after: Parallel rendition + segment jobs; idempotent and retryable.
recap.newRisk: Renditions exist but viewers need adaptive delivery, not raw files.

failureDrills.expectedBehavior: The lease on its rendition_tasks row expires; another worker re-claims the row via the same atomic CAS and re-runs. The deterministic output key means the re-run overwrites the dead attempt's partial output, so nothing is double-counted.
failureDrills.mitigation: Lease held on the rendition_tasks row in MetaDB (queue messages are only wake-ups); atomic CAS claim + lease_until reclaim; idempotent tasks keyed by (video, rendition, segment) with deterministic output keys.

failureDrills.scenario: One segment keeps failing (e.g. a corrupt source region).
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: After max attempts the task moves to dead_letter with an error_code; the rendition is marked failed without blocking other renditions, and an operator/alert is notified.
failureDrills.mitigation: Per-task attempt cap → dead-letter; per-rendition state so one failure doesn't fail the whole video.

flows.name: Upload -> async transcode pipeline
flows.note: The upload returns fast once the source is stored; transcoding happens asynchronously via the queue and worker fleet, marking renditions ready one by one.

flows.name: Worker crash -> lease reclaim -> retry / dead-letter
flows.note: A queue message wakes a worker, which claims the rendition_tasks row in MetaDB via an atomic CAS lease (the row, not the queue message, is the source of truth). If the worker dies, the lease expires and another worker re-claims the row and re-runs idempotently; a task that keeps failing past its attempt cap is dead-lettered and its rendition marked failed without blocking others.

messages.label: lease expires → another worker re-CAS-claims the row
all segments done → validate checksum, mark rendition ready

steps.title: 5. Adaptive Bitrate Streaming via CDN
description: Renditions are segmented (a few seconds each) with an HLS/DASH manifest listing the available qualities. The player downloads the manifest, then fetches segments, switching rendition up/down to match the viewer's bandwidth.

Keep the control plane and data plane separate. The playback service is control plane: it authorizes the viewer by reading the video's visibility and access_policies (public/unlisted/private, geo allow/deny lists, and — for private videos — a check against the creator-owned share ACL) before returning the manifest plus short-TTL signed segment URLs or a signed cookie. It is never on the per-segment hot path. The player then fetches segments directly from the CDN; the CDN caches them near viewers and origin-fetches from the rendition store only on a miss. So a single playback request hits the service once, but the millions of segment fetches never touch it.

CDN specifics that make this work: the cache key is the rendition+segment path (the signing token is excluded from the key, so signed-but-equivalent requests still share a cache entry); segments are immutable so TTLs are long, while manifests get short TTLs; an origin shield tier collapses many edge misses into one origin fetch for long-tail content. Private/unlisted videos stay cacheable without leaking because access is gated by the short-lived token the service mints per authorized viewer — the bytes are public-cacheable but only reachable with a valid token, and tokens expire fast enough that a leaked URL is low-value.
This is the metadata/blob/CDN split: small manifest/metadata from the service, large segments from object storage via CDN edge.

view.caption: Control plane: the viewer requests playback once from the playback service, which fetches the manifest from metadata and returns short-TTL tokenized URLs. Data plane: the viewer then pulls segments directly from the streaming CDN, which origin-fetches from the rendition store only on a miss — the playback service is never in the segment path.

options.name: Adaptive bitrate over CDN, pull-through origin (default)
options.description: Pre-segmented HLS/DASH renditions are served through a CDN that caches hot segments and pulls cold ones from the rendition store, letting players switch rungs as bandwidth changes.
pros: Pre-segmented renditions (HLS/DASH) let the player step down a rung when bandwidth drops, so playback stays smooth.
The CDN caches hot segments and pulls cold ones from the rendition store on first miss, so popular videos rarely hit origin.
Players adapt automatically without server involvement once segments are cached.
cons: Cold or long-tail content suffers an origin-pull latency on the first request.
Storing multiple rendition ladders plus segment manifests multiplies storage.
Cache-key and manifest correctness across renditions is fiddly to get right.

view.caption: The playback service returns signed segment URLs for HLS/DASH renditions, and the viewer fetches them from the CDN, which caches hot segments and pulls cold ones from the rendition store.

options.name: Proactively push popular content to edges
options.description: A popularity predictor pre-warms expected-hot videos into edge caches ahead of demand, so even the first viewers get a cache hit with no origin-pull stall.
pros: Pre-warm new/expected-viral videos to edge caches so even the first viewers get a cache hit — no origin-pull stall.
Smooths origin load for predictable spikes (premieres, trending creators).
Best first-play experience for content you know will be hot.
cons: Wasteful for the long tail — most videos never get enough views to justify pushing everywhere.
Needs a popularity predictor and push pipeline to decide what/where to warm.
Edge storage is finite, so aggressive pushing evicts other hot content.

view.caption: The viewer fetches segments from pre-warmed CDN edges seeded ahead of demand, falling back to an origin fetch from the rendition store only for content not yet pushed.

options.name: Progressive download (single file, no ABR)
options.description: A single MP4 per video is served via byte-range requests with no manifests, segments, or rendition ladder, and the player cannot switch quality mid-stream.
pros: Serve one MP4 via byte-range requests — simplest possible playback path.
No manifests, segmenting, or rendition ladder to manage.
Fine for short clips on stable connections.
cons: No mid-stream quality switch, so a bandwidth drop causes rebuffering — fails the core requirement.
Picking one bitrate punishes either mobile (too high) or desktop (too low).
Seeking and CDN caching are coarser than with small segments.

view.caption: The playback service serves a single progressive MP4 via the CDN over byte ranges, with no manifest or rendition ladder, so the viewer cannot switch quality.

steps.decisionPrompt: A viewer's bandwidth drops mid-video. How does playback stay smooth?

concepts.definition: Segmented renditions + a manifest let the player pick quality per segment.
concepts.whyItMatters: Smooth playback across varying networks without rebuffering.
concepts.example: Player drops from 1080p to 480p segments when throughput falls.

whyNow: Adaptive streaming + CDN is how serving scales to millions of viewers; it's the read-path payoff of the whole pipeline.

recap.after: Viewers stream adaptively from CDN edge.
recap.newRisk: View counts at this scale can't be a synchronous DB increment.

flows.name: Adaptive playback via CDN
flows.note: The player gets a manifest + signed URLs, then streams segments from the CDN edge, switching rendition to match bandwidth; the origin is hit only on a cache miss.

steps.title: 6. View Counting at Scale
description: A synchronous counter increment per view would hammer the database for popular videos. Instead, emit view events into an async pipeline that dedups and aggregates them into counts.

Define what a view actually is. A view event is (video_id, session/device, playback_start, watched_seconds, event_time, request_id). A view is only counted once watched_seconds crosses a threshold (e.g. 30s, or % of a short clip) — that prevents accidental autoplay/refresh inflation. Dedup per (video, session) within a window so replays and refreshes don't double-count; request_id makes retried ingests idempotent. Events are windowed by event_time, and the pipeline tolerates late arrivals (a watermark + grace period) before sealing a window.

Add bot/fraud filtering before counting: drop events failing simple signals (no engagement, datacenter IPs, impossible rates). The public display count is eventually consistent and approximate — acceptable for a view counter — and the pipeline absorbs spikes from viral videos.

Separate the public count from authoritative counts. Recommendations, abuse detection, and especially monetization/ads need stricter, audited, longer-retention records and may apply different validity rules — so the fast approximate public counter and the slower exact analytics/monetization pipeline are deliberately different paths, even if both start from the same event stream.

view.caption: View events from viewers flow into the view-count pipeline, which updates the count through the metadata service into the metadata store.

options.name: Stream-aggregate then batch-write counts (default)
options.description: View events flow into a pipeline that aggregates per video over a time window and writes periodic count deltas, collapsing the firehose into a few writes per minute.
pros: View events flow into a pipeline that aggregates per video over a window and writes periodic deltas, so 1M views/min becomes a few writes/min.
Decouples the spike from the database — the view pipeline absorbs the firehose.
Naturally supports dedup and bot filtering before counting.
cons: The displayed count lags real time by the aggregation window.
Pipeline state must be durable so a restart doesn't lose or double-count windows.
More moving parts than a direct increment.

view.caption: The view-count pipeline aggregates view events per video over a window and writes periodic count deltas to the metadata service, collapsing the firehose into few writes.

options.name: Increment a counter row per view
options.description: Each view issues one UPDATE that increments a counter row stored next to the video metadata, keeping the displayed count exact and always current.
pros: Trivially exact and always up to date — one UPDATE per view.
No pipeline to build; the count lives right next to the video metadata.
Fine for low-traffic videos.
cons: A viral video's hot counter row is a write hotspot that serializes and melts down at 1M/min.
Every view touching the metadata DB couples read and write paths.
No room for dedup/bot filtering before the write.

view.caption: Each viewer's view directly increments a counter row in the metadata service's metadata store, keeping the displayed count exact and current.

options.name: Sharded/approximate counters
options.description: A video's count is spread across N shard counters (or probabilistic structures) so writes fan out instead of contending on one row, and reads sum the shards.
pros: Spread a video's count across N shard counters (or probabilistic structures) so writes don't contend on one row.
Absorbs viral write spikes by fanning increments across keys.
Sum shards on read; exactness can be relaxed for the public display count.
cons: Reads must fan in across shards (or accept an approximate value).
Choosing shard count trades write contention against read fan-out.
Approximation may be unacceptable where exact counts matter (billing, payouts).

view.caption: The view-count pipeline spreads each video's count across many shard counters in the metadata store so writes fan out instead of contending, and reads sum the shards.

steps.decisionPrompt: A video goes viral with 1M views/min. Why not increment a counter row per view?
whyNow: View counting is the classic high-volume-counter subproblem; framing it as a dedup'd async pipeline is the expected answer.

concepts.definition: Aggregate dedup'd events offline instead of incrementing synchronously.
concepts.whyItMatters: Avoids a write hotspot on popular items and absorbs spikes.
concepts.example: View events -> dedup by session -> windowed count -> write back.

recap.newRisk: Storage cost of source + many renditions, and processing reliability at fleet scale.

flows.name: View ingest -> dedup/window -> count writeback
flows.note: View events are fire-and-forget into the pipeline, which dedups per (video, session), gates on the watched-seconds threshold, windows by event_time, and writes periodic count deltas back to metadata. The public approximate counter is written from the fast path; authoritative analytics/monetization branch off the same event stream into a separate, stricter, longer-retention store.

messages.label: bot/fraud filter → dedup (video, session) → threshold gate → window by event_time

steps.title: 7. Storage, Cost, and Reliability at Scale
description: This step synthesizes the reliability and cost mechanisms introduced where they belong (resumable session GC in step 2, the lease/retry/dead-letter task state in step 4, cache/TTL in step 5, dedup/late-event handling in step 6) into the operating picture.

Storage is dominated by sources + renditions. Keep the immutable source (for re-transcode if codecs change), use cheaper/colder storage tiers for old or rarely-watched content, and consider dropping rarely-used renditions.
Make the pipeline reliable end to end: idempotent retryable tasks, dead-letter for repeatedly-failing segments, and per-rendition status so a single failed rendition doesn't block the others.

Observe it where users feel it: queue age / transcode backlog, transcode latency percentiles, per-rendition failure rate, playback start latency and rebuffering ratio, CDN hit ratio and origin egress, and upload completion rate. These are the SLO signals that drive autoscaling and alerting.
Scale ingestion (upload + transcode fleet) and serving (CDN + playback) independently, since their load profiles differ.

view.caption: Transcode workers write renditions to the rendition store and mark them ready in metadata, and the streaming CDN origin-fetches from that store to serve viewers.

steps.decisionPrompt: Renditions dominate storage cost. Which do you keep hot, cold, or drop?
whyNow: Closing on storage/cost/reliability shows the pipeline is operable and economical, not just functional.

recap.after: Cost-tiered storage; reliable, independently-scalable ingestion and serving.
recap.newRisk: Codec migrations and re-transcode of the back catalog — bounded by keeping the immutable source.

bottlenecks.mitigation: Autoscale workers from queue depth; prioritize by creator tier.
Cold-tier old content; lazily generate rare renditions on demand.

finalDesign.title: Final Design — Video Upload & Streaming Pipeline
finalDesign.description: Creators upload sources via resumable chunked upload (session + part state, committed on /complete) to durable storage; the metadata service creates the video as 'processing' and enqueues transcode jobs. Workers fan out renditions (segment-parallel for long videos) over lease-based, idempotent, dead-letterable tasks whose source of truth is the rendition_tasks table in MetaDB (queue messages are only wake-ups; workers claim rows by atomic CAS), write segmented HLS/DASH output to the rendition store, and a coordinator validates and marks renditions ready. The playback service is control plane only: it authorizes the viewer against the video's visibility + access_policies (public/unlisted/private, geo, share ACL) and returns a manifest plus short-TTL tokenized URLs; viewers then stream segments directly from the CDN (origin-shielded over the rendition store) — the service is never on the segment hot path. View events flow through an async, deduped, threshold-gated counting pipeline, with the public approximate counter kept separate from authoritative analytics/monetization records. The state surfaces that make this real: upload session/part state, transcode task/coordinator state, manifest/segment metadata, view-event aggregation state, and operational SLO monitoring. Source + renditions are cost-tiered; ingestion and serving scale independently.

view.caption: The creator uploads resumably to the upload service into the raw store and metadata service, which enqueues transcode jobs for workers that write renditions; viewers request playback, fetch segments from the CDN backed by the rendition store, and emit view events to the view-count pipeline.

functional.how: Resumable chunked upload to durable storage.

nonFunctional.how: The playback service reads visibility + access_policies (geo allow/deny, private-share ACL) before authorizing, then mints a short-TTL token so segments stay CDN-cacheable; bytes are reachable only with a valid token.

say: Confirm it's upload + transcode + streaming, processing is async.
Pin resumable upload, durability, and global CDN playback.
Establish ingestion and serving scale separately.
Resumable chunked upload to object storage.
Decouple with a transcode job queue + workers.
Fan out renditions; serve via CDN with adaptive bitrate.
Segment-parallel transcoding for long videos; idempotent jobs.
Adaptive streaming (HLS/DASH) and the metadata/blob/CDN split.
View counting via async dedup pipeline; storage cost tiering.
Tradeoffs: rendition count vs cost, transcode latency vs parallelism, eventual view counts.
Mention recommendations and live streaming as extensions.

expectations: Uploads to storage and transcodes via a queue.
May miss resumability, segment parallelism, and view-count scale.
Designs resumable upload and async transcode fanout.
Reasons about segment-parallel transcoding and idempotent jobs.
Designs storage cost tiering and codec re-transcode.
Discusses independent scaling of ingestion vs serving precisely.

followUps: How would you support live streaming (low-latency, no full pre-transcode)?
How do you handle a codec migration that requires re-transcoding the back catalog?
How would you generate thumbnails and previews in the same pipeline?
How do you protect content (DRM / signed URLs) without hurting CDN cacheability?
How would you prioritize transcoding for popular creators during a backlog?

links.groupDescription: Large-object upload mechanics, resumability, integrity, and direct-to-storage ingest patterns.

links.groupDescription: Large-object upload mechanics, resumability, integrity, and direct-to-storage ingest patterns.

links.groupDescription: Large-object upload mechanics, resumability, integrity, and direct-to-storage ingest patterns.

links.groupDescription: Large-object upload mechanics, resumability, integrity, and direct-to-storage ingest patterns.

links.group: Video Processing and Encoding at Scale
links.groupDescription: Production encoding systems, quality metrics, codec tradeoffs, and compute-efficiency case studies.

links.group: Video Processing and Encoding at Scale
links.groupDescription: Production encoding systems, quality metrics, codec tradeoffs, and compute-efficiency case studies.

links.group: Video Processing and Encoding at Scale
links.groupDescription: Production encoding systems, quality metrics, codec tradeoffs, and compute-efficiency case studies.

links.group: Video Processing and Encoding at Scale
